svg.text: Basic Operation of a Cache Controller

g.data-narration: Welcome to understanding cache controllers - the bridge between fast processors and slower memory.
g.text: Memory Hierarchy & Performance Optimization in Microcontroller Systems

g.data-narration: Cache memory solves the speed gap between CPU and main memory using hardware that predicts data needs.
Cache works on two key principles: temporal locality - reusing data soon, and spatial locality - accessing nearby data.
The cache controller manages hit/miss detection, tag comparison, data retrieval, and block replacement operations.
Three mapping techniques determine how main memory blocks are placed in cache: direct, fully associative, and set-associative.
In direct mapping, each memory block maps to exactly one cache line using the index field of the address.
Set-associative cache divides lines into sets; each memory block maps to a specific set but can go in any way.
Write policies determine how data updates are handled: write-through writes to both cache and memory; write-back delays memory write.
g.text: Write to Cache AND Main Memory
Write to Cache only, Memory later
Dirty Bit = 1: Modified, write back needed
Dirty Bit = 0: Unmodified, safe to evict

g.data-narration: This example shows write-back operation: data is modified in cache, then written to memory only during eviction.
MESI protocol maintains cache coherence with four states: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, and Invalid for multiprocessor systems.
For address 0x00001000 with 256B cache and 64B blocks: offset is 6 bits, index is 2 bits, tag is 24 bits.
g.text: → 4 Lines, Offset=6 bits, Index=2 bits, Tag=24 bits

g.data-narration: Average Memory Access Time equals hit time plus miss rate multiplied by miss penalty - key performance metric.
g.text: AMAT = Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: cache controllers use locality principles, mapping techniques balance performance and hardware cost.
g.text: Cache bridges CPU speed gap using

g.data-narration: For exams: remember AMAT formula, MESI states, address division calculations, and miss type categories.
